title: Lines & planes in space
summary: A plane in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is one linear equation $ax+by+cz=d$ with normal $\vec n=(a,b,c)$; a line is the intersection of two planes, with direction $\vec p=\vec n_1\times\vec n_2$. This topic builds the Cartesian and parametric forms of both, the parallelism/orthogonality conditions (comparing normals and direction vectors), and the method for every intersection ÔÇö planeÔłęplane, lineÔłęplane, lineÔłęline ÔÇö including the uniquely 3D case of skew lines and orthogonal projection of a point onto a plane.
objectives: Write planes and lines in $\mathbb{R}^3$ in both Cartesian and parametric form, and convert between them
Identify the normal vector of a plane and the direction vector of a line, and use the cross product $\vec n_1\times\vec n_2$ for a line of intersection
Apply the six parallelism/orthogonality conditions for planeÔÇôplane, lineÔÇôplane, and lineÔÇôline pairs
Determine the mutual position of two planes, a line and a plane, or two lines (including detecting skew lines)
Decide whether a line lies in a plane or is strictly parallel to it
Compute intersections by solving the appropriate linear system or by parametric substitution
Find the orthogonal projection of a point onto a plane

blocks.text: Why lines and planes need different equations

In $\mathbb{R}^3$ a **plane** is a flat 2-dimensional surface, and a **line** is 1-dimensional. The professor's key principle: a plane is the solution set of **one** linear equation, while a line is the solution set of a **system of two** independent linear equations. The notes warn: *thinking that a line in three-space is given by one equation is one of the worst things you can do in this class!* One equation always gives a plane; you need two to pin down a line.

A plane $\pi$ is the set of points $(x,y,z)$ satisfying a single linear equation
$$ax+by+cz=d,$$
with $a,b,c$ not all zero. The vector $\vec n=(a,b,c)$ is the **normal vector**: it is orthogonal to the whole plane.

Writing the plane equation as $\vec v\cdot\vec n=d$ (where $\vec v=(x,y,z)$) shows its meaning. If $\vec v_0$ is one fixed point of the plane, then $\vec v_0\cdot\vec n=d$, so $(\vec v-\vec v_0)\cdot\vec n=0$: every displacement inside the plane is perpendicular to $\vec n$. That is why a plane through $P_0$ with normal $\vec n$ is exactly the set of points $P$ with $\overrightarrow{P_0P}\perp\vec n$.

A plane can also be written with **two** free parameters $s,t\in\mathbb{R}$:
$$\vec v=\vec v_0+s\,\vec p+t\,\vec q,$$
where $\vec v_0$ is a point of the plane and $\vec p,\vec q$ are two non-proportional vectors **parallel** to the plane. As $s,t$ range over $\mathbb{R}$ you sweep out the whole plane.

blocks.term: Equation of a line in space
blocks.text:
A line $r$ is the solution set of a system of two independent plane equations (its **Cartesian** form):
$$r:\begin{cases}a_1x+b_1y+c_1z=d_1\\ a_2x+b_2y+c_2z=d_2\end{cases}$$
Its **parametric** form uses one parameter $t$:
$$\vec v=\vec v_0+t\,\vec p,\qquad \vec p\neq\vec 0,$$
where $\vec v_0$ is a point on the line and $\vec p$ is the **direction vector**.

blocks.caption: The direction of a line $r=\pi_1\cap\pi_2$ is the cross product of the two plane normals: since $r$ lies in both planes it is perpendicular to both normals, so $\vec p$ is a multiple of $\vec n_1\times\vec n_2$.

blocks.title: From Cartesian to parametric (line as intersection of two planes)

steps.label: Direction
steps.text: Compute $\vec p=\vec n_1\times\vec n_2$ where $\vec n_1,\vec n_2$ are the two plane normals. (Equivalently, solve the system by elimination and read off the direction.)

steps.label: One point
steps.text: Find one point $\vec v_0$ on the line, e.g. set $z=0$ and solve the remaining $2\times2$ system for $x,y$.

steps.label: Write it
steps.text: $\vec v=\vec v_0+t\,\vec p$. Note a line has no unique parametric form: different valid choices of point/direction give different-looking answers.

blocks.title: Line as intersection of two planes
blocks.text:
Find the parametric form of $r:\begin{cases}x+y+z=1\\ x+2y+3z=4\end{cases}$.

**Direction:** $\vec p=(1,1,1)\times(1,2,3)=(1,-2,1)$.

**Point:** set $z=0$: $x+y=1$, $x+2y=4\Rightarrow y=3,\ x=-2$. So $\vec v_0=(-2,3,0)$.

**Answer:** $\vec v=(-2,3,0)+t(1,-2,1)$, i.e. $x=-2+t,\ y=3-2t,\ z=t$.

(Check by elimination: subtracting the equations gives $y+2z=3$, so $y=-2z+3$ and $x=z-2$; setting $z=t$ reproduces the same line.)

To go the **other way** (parametric to Cartesian for a plane): take the two direction vectors $\vec p,\vec q$, set $\vec n=\vec p\times\vec q$, write $ax+by+cz=d$ with $(a,b,c)=\vec n$, and find $d$ by plugging in the known point $\vec v_0$.

Everything reduces to comparing **normals** (planes) and **direction vectors** (lines), using $\parallel$ for "proportional" and $\perp$ for "dot product zero". This is the heart of every exam question on mutual position.

blocks.tex: \begin{aligned}&\alpha\parallel\beta \iff \vec n_\alpha\parallel\vec n_\beta &&\alpha\perp\beta \iff \vec n_\alpha\perp\vec n_\beta\\ &\alpha\parallel r \iff \vec n_\alpha\perp\vec v_r &&\alpha\perp r \iff \vec n_\alpha\parallel\vec v_r\\ &r\parallel s \iff \vec v_r\parallel\vec v_s &&r\perp s \iff \vec v_r\perp\vec v_s\end{aligned}
blocks.caption: The six parallelism/orthogonality conditions. Note the swap for a line vs a plane: a line is parallel to a plane when its direction is perpendicular to the normal.

blocks.text: **Plane vs line is reversed.** Two planes are parallel when their normals are parallel, but a line is parallel to a plane when its direction is **perpendicular** to the normal ($\vec n_\alpha\cdot\vec v_r=0$). A line is perpendicular to a plane when its direction is **parallel** to the normal. Mixing these up is the most common mistake on these problems.

blocks.term: Line lies in a plane vs is parallel to it
blocks.text: If $\vec n_\alpha\cdot\vec v_r=0$ (direction $\perp$ normal), the line is either **parallel to** the plane or **contained in** it. To decide: test whether one point of the line satisfies the plane equation. If yes $\Rightarrow$ the line lies **in** the plane; if no $\Rightarrow$ the line is **strictly parallel** (no intersection).
Intersections and the size of the system
Intersecting objects means solving the combined system of their equations. The number of equations tells you the system size, and the geometry tells you how many solutions to expect.

blocks.tex: \begin{array}{ll}\pi_1\cap\pi_2: & 2\times3\ \text{system (line, or empty/coincident if }\vec n_1\parallel\vec n_2)\\ \alpha\cap r: & 3\times3\ \text{system (a point, unless }\vec n_\alpha\perp\vec v_r)\\ r\cap s: & 4\times3\ \text{system (point if coplanar, empty if skew)}\end{array}
blocks.caption: System sizes for the three basic intersections.

blocks.text: **Plane $\cap$ plane:** if normals are proportional the planes are parallel (no solution) or coincide (a whole plane of solutions); otherwise the intersection is a line. **Plane $\cap$ line:** if $\vec n_\alpha\perp\vec v_r$ then either no point (parallel) or the line lies in the plane (infinitely many); if $\vec n_\alpha\cdot\vec v_r\neq0$ there is exactly **one** intersection point. **Line $\cap$ line:** if directions are proportional the lines are parallel; if not, they either meet in one point (coplanar) or never meet ÔÇö **skew lines**, a feature unique to $\mathbb{R}^3$.

steps.label: Parametrize the line
steps.text: Write $r$ as $(x,y,z)=\vec v_0+t\,\vec p$, giving $x,y,z$ in terms of one parameter $t$.

steps.label: Substitute
steps.text: Plug those expressions into the single plane equation $ax+by+cz=d$. You get one equation in $t$.

steps.label: Solve and back-substitute
steps.text: Solve for $t$; if there is a unique $t$, put it back into the line to get the point. No solution $\Rightarrow$ parallel; identity $0=0$ $\Rightarrow$ line lies in the plane.

blocks.title: Line meets a plane in one point
blocks.text:
Plane $\alpha:x+y+z=0$ and line $r:\begin{cases}y-x=1\\ z-y=1\end{cases}$.

Set $y=t$: then $x=t-1$ and $z=t+1$, so $(x,y,z)=(-1,0,1)+t(1,1,1)$.

Substitute into $\alpha$: $(t-1)+(t)+(t+1)=0\Rightarrow 3t=0\Rightarrow t=0$.

Intersection point: $(-1,0,1)$. (Here $\vec n_\alpha\cdot\vec p=(1,1,1)\cdot(1,1,1)=3\neq0$, confirming a single point.)

Two lines are **skew** if they are neither parallel (directions not proportional) nor intersecting (system has no solution). They do not lie in a common plane. In $\mathbb{R}^2$ this is impossible ÔÇö two lines in the plane are always either parallel or intersecting ÔÇö so skewness is a genuinely 3-dimensional phenomenon.

blocks.title: Detecting a skew pair
blocks.text:
Lines $l:x=y=z$, direction $\vec p_l=(1,1,1)$, and $s:(x,y,z)=t(6,3,2)+(1,2,3)$, direction $\vec p_s=(6,3,2)$.

Directions are not proportional, so they are not parallel. Intersect using $x=y=z$ on $s$: $6t+1=3t+2=2t+3$. From $6t+1=3t+2$ we get $t=\tfrac13$; from $3t+2=2t+3$ we get $t=1$. Contradiction $\Rightarrow$ no common point.

Not parallel and not intersecting $\Rightarrow$ **$l$ and $s$ are skew.**

Decision tree for two lines: (1) directions proportional? $\to$ **parallel** (or coincident). (2) Not proportional but system solvable? $\to$ **intersecting** (coplanar). (3) Not proportional and system unsolvable? $\to$ **skew**.

question.prompt: How many independent linear equations are needed to describe a line in $\mathbb{R}^3$?
question.explanation: A single linear equation always describes a plane (2-dimensional). A line is 1-dimensional and needs the intersection of two independent planes, i.e. two equations. Three would generically over-determine to a point or be empty.

question.prompt: A line $r$ has direction $\vec v_r=(1,2,1)$ and a plane $\alpha$ has normal $\vec n=(1,-1,1)$. What is the relation between $r$ and $\alpha$?

options.content: $r$ is parallel to or contained in $\alpha$
Cannot be determined from this data

question.explanation: Compute $\vec n\cdot\vec v_r=(1)(1)+(-1)(2)+(1)(1)=1-2+1=0$. Direction is perpendicular to the normal, so the line is parallel to the plane (or lies inside it ÔÇö a point test would decide). A is wrong (that needs $\vec v_r\parallel\vec n$); C is wrong (that needs $\vec n\cdot\vec v_r\neq0$); the relation IS determined, so D is wrong.
question.theory: Parallelism of a line and a plane.
